evals.name: library_choice_casual
evals.prompt: need to pick a background job library for our .NET 8 api, hangfire vs quartz vs whatever else is good these days. we're on postgres and want a dashboard
evals.expected_output: A Comparative-mode research brief. Verifies current library status against live sources (staleness gate — 'these days' is explicitly time-sensitive), builds an options matrix scored against the stated constraints (.NET 8, Postgres storage, dashboard), includes a risk register, a committed recommendation, and a 'What I Would Do' section, then hands off to a design/implementation skill.
assertions: Treated the question as time-sensitive and verified library status/versions against live sources rather than answering purely from training knowledge
Options table evaluates against the user's actual constraints (Postgres, dashboard, .NET 8), not abstractly
Every finding carries a source and a VERIFIED/ASSUMED/OPINION label
Risk register present with evidence-backed risks (e.g. maintenance activity, licensing changes) or an explicit 'None identified'
'What I Would Do' section makes one committed first-person call, not a restatement of options
Named a handoff skill and what it needs from the brief

evals.name: fact_check_stale_claim
evals.prompt: a blog post I read says you can't use output caching with authenticated endpoints in ASP.NET Core, is that actually true?
evals.expected_output: A Quick-mode fact-check that treats the claim as version-dependent, checks current official documentation, dates the finding, and answers directly with the version context — including whether the blog's claim was ever true and for which versions.
assertions: Checked official Microsoft documentation rather than settling the question from training knowledge or other blogs
Answer is version-qualified (states which ASP.NET Core versions the answer applies to)
Distinguishes what the source claims from what the evidence shows
If verification was impossible, the answer is explicitly flagged as unverified training knowledge rather than presented as fact

evals.name: honest_about_unverified
evals.prompt: quick one, what's the current rate limit on the GitHub REST API for authenticated requests? no need to go deep
evals.expected_output: Either a live-verified answer with source and date, or — if not verified — the training-knowledge figure explicitly flagged as possibly stale with a pointer to the authoritative docs page. Never an unflagged number presented as current fact, even though the user said 'no need to go deep'.
assertions: Did not present a rate-limit figure as current without either live verification or an explicit staleness flag
Named the authoritative source (GitHub docs) either as the verification or as where to confirm
Stayed Quick mode — did not spawn a multi-agent deep research run for a single fact

evals.name: should_not_trigger_plan_request
evals.prompt: ok we're going with Hangfire, break the integration down into phases for me
evals.expected_output: Does NOT run a research pass. The decision is made; routes to planner to produce the staged plan.
assertions: Did not produce a research brief or re-open the already-made decision
Routed to or applied the planner skill

evals.name: should_not_trigger_stable_knowledge
evals.prompt: what's the time complexity of quicksort in the worst case and why?
evals.expected_output: A direct answer from training knowledge (O(n²), pivot degeneration) — time-stable fact, no web research, no research brief scaffolding.
assertions: Answered directly without spawning research subagents or web searches
Did not wrap the answer in the research brief format
